                King's Indian Attack
                Bg2c6
                
        

*King's Indian Defence

* Flank Openings (1990), Bruno Carlier, Trends Publications, No ISBN.

A sound, but very unambitious opening move where white almost certainly intends to fianchetto his bishop and enable castling. Black has the choice to continue to mirror white's move once more with g6 or begin a more aggressive attempt to control the center.
Nf6, keeping the position symmetrical, is the most flexible reply to 1. At Grandmaster level this is a very popular move and has been the preferred response of both Anand and Kasparov.
Nf3 is the third most popular initial move. It is highly flexible and often transposes into lines better known through other move orders, though some independent lines are possible.
b4 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the King's Indian Attack.

variations.name: Sicilian Variation

annotation.moveRationale: Re1 repositions the rook toward an open or key file in the King's Indian Attack.
Castles kingside, securing the king and connecting the rooks in the King's Indian Attack.
Castles kingside, securing the king and connecting the rooks in the King's Indian Attack.
Be7 develops the bishop to a modest but solid square, preparing to castle in the King's Indian Attack.
Bg2 develops a piece to an active square in the King's Indian Attack.
Nc6 develops toward the center, supporting the pawn structure in the King's Indian Attack.
g3 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the King's Indian Attack.
c5 challenges the center from the flank in the King's Indian Attack.
Ngf3 develops a piece to an active square in the King's Indian Attack.
Nf6 develops the knight while attacking the e4 pawn in the King's Indian Attack.

With Nd2 White transposes to the King's Indian Attack. The next moves for white are usually Ngf3, g3, Bg2, O-O and Re1, preparing to fight for the center but also looking at the long term plan of directly attacking the king.
Black continues with the mainline French moves whereas white is preparing to enter a King's Indian Attack setup after Nd2. White can also transpose to other openings.

This is a way for White to avoid the "classical" French Defence. Though White's game looks less active (White will have to fianchetto his king's bishop, leaving time for Black to develop), a powerful attack is possible by advancing the king's pawn to e5, building a strong outpost.
e6 is the French defence. Black plans to gain space in the centre with their d pawn.

__NOTOC__

Best by test. — Bobby Fischer

1.
